questions.question: What is a fractal?
questions.correct_answer: A geometric figure that shows self-similarity at different scales
questions.explanation: Fractals repeat similar patterns at smaller and larger scales.

questions.question: What does self-similarity mean in fractals?
questions.correct_answer: Small parts of the figure resemble the whole figure
questions.explanation: Fractals repeat the same structure at different sizes.

questions.question: What shape is used to start the Sierpinski Triangle?
questions.explanation: The fractal begins with a triangle which is repeatedly subdivided.

questions.question: How is the Sierpinski Carpet formed?
questions.correct_answer: By dividing a square into 9 smaller squares and removing the central square repeatedly
questions.explanation: This process is repeated recursively to create a fractal pattern.

questions.question: Which geometric figure is used to start the Koch Snowflake?
questions.explanation: Each side of the triangle is modified repeatedly to create the fractal boundary.

questions.question: What happens to the perimeter of the Koch Snowflake after each step?
questions.explanation: New segments are added at each iteration.

questions.question: What is a polyhedron?
questions.correct_answer: A three-dimensional solid with flat polygonal faces
questions.explanation: Examples include cubes, prisms, and pyramids.

questions.question: What are the three main elements of a polyhedron?
questions.explanation: Faces are flat surfaces, edges connect faces, and vertices are intersection points.

questions.question: What is a net of a solid?
questions.correct_answer: A flat two-dimensional pattern that folds to form a three-dimensional solid
questions.explanation: Nets help visualize how faces of a solid are connected.

questions.question: What is projection in geometry?
questions.correct_answer: A representation of a three-dimensional object on a two-dimensional plane
questions.explanation: It shows how an object appears from a particular direction.

notes.title: Fractals
notes.content: Fractals are geometric patterns that repeat themselves at different scales and are often generated through recursive processes.

notes.title: Sierpinski Figures
notes.content: Sierpinski Triangle and Sierpinski Carpet are examples of fractals created by repeatedly removing parts of geometric shapes.

notes.title: Koch Snowflake
notes.content: A fractal curve formed by repeatedly modifying the sides of an equilateral triangle.

notes.title: Nets of Solids
notes.content: Nets are flat patterns that can be folded to create three-dimensional solids.

notes.title: Projections and Isometric Drawings
notes.content: These techniques help represent three-dimensional objects accurately on two-dimensional surfaces.

facts.fact: A cube has 6 faces, 12 edges and 8 vertices.
Fractals are used in computer graphics to generate realistic natural patterns.
A cube has 11 different possible nets.
The isometric projection of a cube appears as a hexagon outline.
